name: neg_6_disjointness
property: allocated_addrs and free_addrs are not necessarily disjoint
reason: An address in both sets means it is treated as both allocated and free — a use-after-free vulnerability
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, result: Result<*mut u8, Error>

name: neg_6_disjointness_fresh_slab
property: allocated_addrs and free_addrs are not necessarily disjoint
reason: If disjointness is not preserved even from a trivially-disjoint initial state, the invariant is fundamentally broken
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, result: Result<*mut u8, Error>

name: neg_6_disjointness_after_cycle
property: allocated_addrs and free_addrs are not necessarily disjoint
reason: Alloc-dealloc cycling could introduce an address into both sets if the invariant does not enforce disjointness
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, result: Result<*mut u8, Error>

name: neg_6_disjointness_interleaved
property: allocated_addrs and free_addrs are not necessarily disjoint
reason: Multiple interleaved alloc/dealloc operations are the most likely scenario to expose set overlap if inv() is weak
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, result: Result<*mut u8, Error>

name: neg_7_unaligned_dealloc
property: deallocate succeeds for a pointer that is not aligned to block_size
reason: An unaligned pointer entering free_addrs corrupts the allocator's alignment invariant for future allocations
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, ptr: *const u8, result: Result<(), Error>

name: neg_7_unaligned_dealloc_normal
property: deallocate succeeds for a pointer that is not aligned to block_size
reason: If allocated_addrs only contains aligned addresses AND ptr is in allocated_addrs, unalignment should be contradictory
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, ptr: *const u8, result: Result<(), Error>

name: neg_7_unaligned_dealloc_weak_inv
property: deallocate succeeds for a pointer that is not aligned to block_size
reason: If inv() does not enforce alignment of all addresses in allocated_addrs, unaligned pointers can leak into free_addrs
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, ptr: *const u8, result: Result<(), Error>

name: neg_8_double_free_err_code
property: double-free is not prevented if the same pointer is somehow in allocated_addrs twice (set semantics should prevent this, but the error path allows silent no-ops)
reason: An unconstrained error code on the Err path means callers cannot programmatically detect or handle double-free attempts
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, ptr: *const u8, result: Result<(), Error>

name: neg_8_double_free_err_code_just_freed
property: double-free is not prevented if the same pointer is somehow in allocated_addrs twice (set semantics should prevent this, but the error path allows silent no-ops)
reason: After deallocation, re-freeing the same pointer should produce a specific diagnosable error, not an arbitrary one
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, ptr: *const u8, result: Result<(), Error>

name: neg_8_double_free_err_code_never_allocated
property: double-free is not prevented if the same pointer is somehow in allocated_addrs twice (set semantics should prevent this, but the error path allows silent no-ops)
reason: Freeing a never-allocated pointer is a severe bug that should produce a distinguishable error code
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, ptr: *const u8, result: Result<(), Error>

name: neg_8_double_free_err_code_null
property: double-free is not prevented if the same pointer is somehow in allocated_addrs twice (set semantics should prevent this, but the error path allows silent no-ops)
reason: Freeing a null pointer should produce a specific, documented error code to aid debugging
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, ptr: *const u8, result: Result<(), Error>

name: neg_9_free_count_unconstrained
property: from_raw_parts does not constrain the total number of free blocks relative to the memory region size
reason: A slab reporting 1 free block in a region large enough for 10+ wastes most of the memory region
params: addr: *mut u8, len: usize, block_size: usize, result: Result<Slab, Error>

name: neg_9_free_count_single_block
property: from_raw_parts does not constrain the total number of free blocks relative to the memory region size
reason: When len equals block_size, at most one block fits, but the spec does not bound free_addrs cardinality
params: addr: *mut u8, len: usize, block_size: usize, result: Result<Slab, Error>

name: neg_9_free_count_large_region
property: from_raw_parts does not constrain the total number of free blocks relative to the memory region size
reason: Claiming more free blocks than physically fit in the memory region is a logical impossibility the spec should exclude
params: addr: *mut u8, len: usize, block_size: usize, result: Result<Slab, Error>

name: neg_9_free_count_non_multiple
property: from_raw_parts does not constrain the total number of free blocks relative to the memory region size
reason: Counting a partial trailing block as a full free block would allow allocation of memory beyond the region boundary
params: addr: *mut u8, len: usize, block_size: usize, result: Result<Slab, Error>

name: neg_10_always_same_addr
property: allocate could always pick the same address from free_addrs even when multiple are available
reason: Always returning the same address starves all other free blocks and wastes allocator capacity
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, result: Result<*mut u8, Error>

name: neg_10_always_same_addr_single
property: allocate could always pick the same address from free_addrs even when multiple are available
reason: With a single free block there is no choice — this scenario should trivially verify as a baseline
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, result: Result<*mut u8, Error>

name: neg_10_always_same_addr_multiple
property: allocate could always pick the same address from free_addrs even when multiple are available
reason: When multiple blocks are available, always returning start_addr means subsequent blocks are never used
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, result: Result<*mut u8, Error>

name: neg_10_always_same_addr_recycled
property: allocate could always pick the same address from free_addrs even when multiple are available
reason: Repeated alloc-dealloc on the same block with other blocks available indicates degenerate no-progress behavior
params: pre: Slab, post: Slab, result: Result<*mut u8, Error>
